Tentative Rulings
Text-to-Speech

DEPARTMENT P LAW AND MOTION RULINGS


Case Number: 25NNCV00140    Hearing Date: March 2, 2026    Dept: P

[TENTATIVE] ORDER DENYING MOTION FOR RECONSIDERATION OF THE COURT’S OCTOBER 23, 2025, DISCOVERY ORDER

I.        INTRODUCTION

This is a medical malpractice action arising from spinal surgery performed by Defendant Shane S. Pak, M.D. (“Dr. Pak” or “Defendant”) on Plaintiff Sai K. Wu (“Plaintiff”) at Pacific Orthopaedic Associates (“Pacific”) in September 2023. Plaintiffs Sai K. Wu and Shirley Wu (collectively, “Plaintiffs”) allege that Dr. Pak’s negligent surgical technique and postoperative management caused permanent neurological injury, including foot drop and loss of bladder function. Shirley Wu asserts a derivative claim for loss of consortium.

On October 23, 2025, the Court granted in part and denied in part Plaintiffs’ motion to compel Defendant to further respond to Plaintiffs’ requests for production of documents. As relevant here, the Court denied the motion as to RFPs Nos. 5-8, seeking “transcripts of Dr. Pak’s prior testimony in depositions, trials, arbitrations, and Medical Board proceedings.” (10-23-2025 Minute Order, p. 2.)

On January 13, 2026, Plaintiffs moved for reconsideration of the Court’s October 23, 2025 Order. They argue production of the disputed records is warranted based on Dr. Pak’s conduct at his December 18, 2025 deposition, where he unreasonably disclaimed all memory of any facts having to do with treatment of Plaintiff. Plaintiffs contend there is now good cause to produce records from past proceedings to impeach Dr. Pak’s credibility at trial.

Defendants filed their opposition on February 17, 2026, and on February 23, 2026, Plaintiffs filed their reply.

 

II.      LEGAL STANDARD

“When an application for an order has been made to a judge, or to a court, and refused in whole . . .  any party affected by the order may, within 10 days after service upon the party of written notice of entry of the order and based upon new or different facts, circumstances, or law, make application to the same judge or court that made the order, to reconsider the matter and modify, amend, or revoke the prior order.” (Code Civ. Proc., § 1008, subd., (a).) “When a motion is granted or denied, unless the court otherwise orders, notice of the court’s decision or order shall be given by the prevailing party to all other parties or their attorneys, in the manner provided in this chapter, unless notice is waived by all parties in open court and is entered in the minutes.” (Code Civ. Proc., § 1019.5, subd. (a).)

 

“If a court at any time determines that there has been a change of law that warrants it to reconsider a prior order it entered, it may do so on its own motion and enter a different order.” (Code Civ. Proc., § 1008, subd. (c); see also Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal.4th 1094, 1106-1107.) The permissive time frame of section 1008, subdivision (c) distinguishes it from subdivision (a), which requires a party moving for reconsideration to give notice within ten days of the challenged order. (Ibid.; compare Code Civ. Proc., § 1008, subd. (a).)

 

          III.     DISCUSSION       

          The motion is denied.

          First: regardless of when new evidence is discovered, section 1008 requires a motion to reconsider to be filed within 10 days of the challenged order. (Code Civ. Proc., § 1008(a).) Plaintiffs filed their motion in January challenging an order from the prior October. The motion is not timely, and it may not be granted. The Court may also not grant the motion pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1008(c), as there has been no relevant change in the law.

          To the extent Plaintiffs rely on authority apart from section 1008, their motion also fails on its merits.

According to Plaintiffs, “Dr. Pak’s testimony in other matters is now highly probative of his ability. to recall and communicate [his] recollections” of patient care generally. (Mot., 2:24-25.) Put differently: if Dr. Pak had a good memory in other cases, then his failure to recall in this case is suspicious and goes to his credibility.

Dr. Pak’s dubious inability to recall facts related to Plaintiffs’ case does not justify changing the Court’s prior ruling.

The Court reasoned as follows in its minute order of October 23, 2025:

“These requests are overly broad in scope, would encompass testimony involving unrelated patients, and pose a substantial privacy concern even with redactions. While ‘fishing expeditions’ may be permissible under some circumstances, they are not appropriate when information protected by privacy rights is involved; rather, Plaintiffs are required to seek only information that they can show to be, at a minimum, ‘directly relevant.’ (See Nelson v. Superior Court (1986) 184 Cal.App.3d 444, 456-457.)”

(10-23-2025 Minute Order, pp. 2-3.)

Deposition testimony from prior cases still implicates other patients’ privacy, notwithstanding Dr. Pak’s stonewalling. And Plaintiffs have not shown good cause to produce any specific information; they speculate that Dr. Pak has behaved differently in other cases. Plaintiffs have also not shown the testimony is “directly relevant” – it is, at best, only relevant to witness credibility. The Court doubts the evidence Plaintiffs seek would be admissible, even for impeachment purposes, since it does not involve the case at bar. A witness’s self-serving inability to recall relevant information is a common strategy in litigation; skilled counsel can easily emphasize this strategy for impeachment purposes without intruding unnecessarily into nonparties’ privacy during discovery.

 

IV.     CONCLUSION

          Plaintiffs’ Motion for Reconsideration is DENIED.

          Counsel for Plaintiffs to give notice of this order.

         

Dated: March 2, 2026
 JARED D. MOSES
JUDGE OF THE SUPERIOR COURT
